2606.05970.title: Measuring the sensitivity of LLM-based structured extraction to prompt, model, and schema choices in clinical discharge summaries
2606.05970.paper_summary: Large language models are increasingly used for structured extraction from clinical free-text notes, but the sensitivity of their output to upstream configuration choices is less understood than their accuracy on fixed benchmarks. This work measures that sensitivity without human-annotated ground truth, by holding the extraction task fixed and varying one choice at a time. The fixed schema comprises 17 clinical documentation flags on a three-way yes/no/not_documented value set and a 47-tag vocabulary for the primary admission reason. Three prompt variants expressing this schema were each run at two model sizes on MIMIC-IV v3.1 discharge summaries. Cross-prompt agreement was measured by Cohen's kappa on ICD-stratified subsets. A paired same-note comparison isolated the effect of model choice, and a post-hoc collapse of the three-way flags to binary tested the schema's contribution to disagreement. On the three-way flags, the two models reach the same pooled cross-prompt agreement (median kappa 0.69 and 0.68); the larger model raises agreement on some fields and lowers it on others, a redistribution rather than the absence of an effect. Collapsing the schema to binary dissolves most of the cross-prompt disagreement, locating it on the absence-versus-silence distinction rather than on whether the finding is present. On the multi-class admission categorization, changing the model reassigns the dominant tag on close to half of all notes while changing the prompt phrasing reassigns it on roughly one in eight, and the larger model places far less mass on residual catch-all categories (44% to 26%). These patterns indicate a schema-imposed source of disagreement concentrated on the absence-versus-silence axis and a dominance of model over prompt phrasing on multi-class categorization, identified by a reusable methodology for auditing extraction reproducibility on a population-scale deployment.
2606.05970.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型越來越多地用於從臨床自由文本筆記中進行結構化提取，但其輸出對上游配置選擇的敏感性比在固定基準上的準確性理解得更少。這項工作在沒有人工標註的真實情況下測量了這種敏感性，通過固定提取任務並一次變更一個選擇來進行。固定的架構包括17個臨床文檔標誌，對應三種值集：是/否/未記錄，以及一個47標籤的主要入院原因詞彙。三個表達此架構的提示變體在MIMIC-IV v3.1出院摘要上以兩種模型大小運行。通過Cohen's kappa在ICD分層子集上測量交叉提示一致性。配對相同筆記的比較隔離了模型選擇的影響，而對三種標誌進行後驗二元化測試了架構對不一致性的貢獻。在三種標誌上，這兩個模型達到相同的合併交叉提示一致性（中位數kappa 0.69和0.68）；較大的模型在某些領域提高了一致性，而在其他領域降低了一致性，這是一種重新分配，而不是缺乏影響。將架構壓縮為二元形式幾乎消除了大部分的交叉提示不一致性，將其定位於缺失與沉默的區別，而不是發現是否存在。在多類別入院分類中，改變模型使接近一半的所有筆記的主導標籤重新分配，而改變提示措辭則使大約八分之一的標籤重新分配，且較大的模型對殘餘的包羅萬象類別的質量分配大大減少（從44%降至26%）。這些模式表明了一種由架構強加的不一致性來源，集中在缺失與沉默的軸上，以及在多類別分類中模型對提示措辭的主導地位，這一點通過可重用的方法論來審核提取的可重複性，適用於人口規模的部署。
2606.05970.author: Martin Murin et.al.
